---
title: "Economists under Constraint: Controversies and the Construction of Climate Finance as an Economic Object (1990–2025)"
author: "[Anonymous]"
keywords:
  - climate finance
  - quantification
  - accounting categories
  - international organisations
jel:
  - B20
  - Q54
  - F35
  - Q56
date: "2026-07-08"
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
metadata-files: [manuscript-vars.yml]
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
csl: bibliography/oeconomia.csl
number-sections: true
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          \usepackage{etoolbox}
          \renewcommand{\CSLBlock}[1]{\newline\noindent #1}
---

<!-- Phase 2→3 contract: this manuscript consumes
     - figures/fig_bars_v1.png          (@fig-bars)
     - figures/fig_breaks.png           (@fig-breaks)
     - figures/fig_composition.png      (@fig-composition)
     - tables/tab_venues.md             (@tbl-venues)
     Any change to these outputs requires rebuilding the manuscript.

     English translation of the frozen French source content/manuscript-Gide.qmd
     (tag gide-submitted-2026-06-29), machine-performed and style-anchored on the
     author's pre-AI English prose (docs/style-anchor-v205.md, ticket 0173). -->

**Abstract.** Since the early 1990s, climate finance has moved from a vague expression of international policy to a central economic object, quantified, compared and audited in the climate negotiations, at the OECD and in the multilateral development banks. Successive collective commitments -- from the goal of $100 billion per year adopted at Copenhagen in 2009 to the new quantified goal of $300 billion by 2035 set at Baku in 2024 -- have made climate finance a nodal point of international climate governance. Yet after three decades of statistical effort, controversies persist over what should be counted as climate finance, how to value it, and how to attribute responsibilities among actors.

We propose a historical analysis of the construction of climate finance as an economic object between 1990 and 2025. It departs from a reading that would make climate finance a mere technical extension of environmental economics, or of a climate economics grounded in integrated modelling. The founding work on externalities and collective environmental constraints [@ayres_kneese1969], then the models centred on growth, damages, risk and time [@nordhaus1992; @manne_richels1992; @stern2007; @weitzman2007], structured part of climate expertise; the question of sharing the international financial effort, however, developed largely outside these frames.

Climate finance emerged mainly through the entanglement of international negotiations, development finance practices, and the accounting tools mobilised to make political commitments operational. At the OECD, and within the Development Assistance Committee in particular, economists such as Jan Corfee-Morlot played a central role in building statistical infrastructures -- Rio markers, grant-equivalent valuation methods, monitoring and reporting frameworks -- which made climate finance visible and measurable within the existing frames of official development assistance [@corfee-morlot2009; @corfee-morlot2012].

These accounting categories soon became sites of controversy within the economics profession. On one side, approaches grounded in the correction of market failures emphasised efficiency, leverage and the mobilisation of private capital, justifying blended finance and de-risking instruments. On the other, economists working from a political-economy perspective contested both the measurement conventions and their normative implications. Axel and Katharina Michaelowa exposed the incentives to over-report inherent in the Rio markers [@michaelowa2007], while Romain Weikmans and J. Timmons Roberts read the recurring controversies as the expression of unresolved distributive conflicts between North and South [@roberts_weikmans2017; @weikmans_roberts2019].

Through four recurring controversies -- the valuation of concessional loans, the credibility of the Rio markers, the attribution of mobilised private finance, and the boundary between development aid and climate obligations -- we show that economists acted as technical experts and, beyond that, as actors at the science-society boundary, permanently redefining what climate finance is and ought to be. Climate finance thus stands out as a privileged case for analysing the political limits of economic quantification in international governance.

**Keywords:** climate finance, quantification, accounting categories, international organisations

**JEL codes:** B20, Q54, F35, Q56

\newpage

## Introduction: a precise number, an unstable object {#sec-intro}

In November 2024, in Baku, climate negotiators adopted a new collective goal: developed countries should mobilise $300 billion per year by 2035 for climate action in developing countries. The number is sharp. The object it designates is much less so. No fund holds these 300 billion. No single ledger records them. No counting method commands assent. The number is produced, like the $100 billion per year announced at Copenhagen in 2009, by applying disputed accounting conventions to heterogeneous financial flows: grants, concessional loans, non-concessional loans, guarantees, equity stakes, private co-financing, multilateral contributions.

This paradox is what makes climate finance an interesting object for the history of economic thought. Climate finance is treated today as a self-evident economic quantity: it is tracked, compared, audited and commented upon in reports from the OECD, the UNFCCC, the multilateral development banks and NGOs. Yet its perimeter remains disputed. Should a loan be counted at face value, or only at its grant equivalent? Can a public guarantee bring the private investment it accompanies into the climate finance total? Does an ordinary development project become climatic because the institution that finances it marks it as such? These questions are key to determining whether a country honours its international commitments, to separating development aid from external debt, and to gauging how new and additional the international effort of climate solidarity really is.

They also put a more general question to the history of economic thought: how can a quantity whose perimeter remains contested become a legitimate economic variable? Climate finance forces us to take seriously quantities that function before being fully defined. It is neither a pure accounting illusion nor a natural variable waiting to be measured. It is an operative category, stabilised by conventions, institutions and reporting practices, yet permanently exposed to controversy.

The thesis defended here is simple: economists did not discover climate finance the way one discovers a reality already there. The financial flows themselves exist: public budgets, loans, guarantees, equity stakes and private investment. What is constructed is their aggregation into a governable quantity. Economists contributed to this construction by devising the accounting categories, the concessionality thresholds, the purpose markers and the attribution methods that make otherwise heterogeneous flows comparable. This work was not carried out in academic journals alone. It played out in international organisations, statistical committees, expert reports, development banks and reporting procedures. The economics at stake here is an economics of measurement conventions, more than one of equilibrium models or cost-benefit calculation.

The phrase in the title -- "economists under constraint" -- is to be taken literally. The constraint is diplomatic first: the amounts are fixed before the object is stabilised. It is also statistical, because the apparatus of official development assistance pre-exists and imposes its categories. It is political, finally: the same instruments must reconcile two poorly compatible requirements, the efficiency of mobilisation and the accountability of transfers. Economists act neither as sovereigns nor as mere executants; they work in this constrained space, where every measurement convention is also a compromise.

The literature on the history of climate economics has mostly studied modelling: intertemporal optimisation, the choice of the discount rate, the value of carbon, integrated assessment models [@pottier2016; @aykut_dahan2015]. These works are essential for understanding how climate change was constituted as an economic problem. They say less about how climate-related financial flows were constituted as a measurable object. The shift matters. Moving from the question "what should the price of carbon be?" to the question "does this dollar count as climate finance?" amounts to changing intellectual regime: from the model to accounting, and from optimisation to commensuration [@espeland_stevens1998; @merry2016]. It also means changing the type of economic rationality: no longer only computing an optimal trajectory, but producing a quantity solid enough to verify political commitments, and supple enough to remain negotiable.

This text orders economic thought on climate finance into three eras. During the first, up to the mid-2000s, several traditions exist separately: environmental economics, development economics, the economics of international effort-sharing. None of them produces climate finance on its own. The second period, 2007--2014, is one of crystallization: Bali and Copenhagen create the demand for measurement, the OECD/DAC and the multilateral banks supply the counting infrastructures. The third era, since 2015, is that of controversies around the existing object. A concrete operation -- a World Bank financing in Türkiye -- serves to show how these controversies become operative. The conclusion returns to the main result: climate finance became governable through the ambiguity of its categories, not despite it.

## Method

This text proposes a history of economic thought outside its canonical sites. It follows the models, the theoretical articles and the academic controversies less than the operative categories through which economists in international organisations, development banks and expert networks made a diplomatic commitment calculable. Climate finance thus appears as a borderline object: neither a simple application of environmental economics nor a simple extension of development aid, but the product of a practical economics of measurement.

The proposed periodization follows from our reading of the texts, from participant observation of the debates, notably on integrated modelling and at the IPCC, and from the analysis of the climate negotiations. To test it, we add a computational analysis of a multilingual corpus on climate finance (@fig-bars). The point is to submit the interpretation to an independent test, short of a falsification attempt under established disciplinary criteria; computation does not replace interpretation here. A quantitative method seemed useful to us to illustrate the argument, at the border between the history of economic thought and science-and-society studies.

This way of doing intellectual history may surprise. The discipline usually reads a small number of canonical texts closely. Yet climate finance has no canon. At least, if a canon exists in the field, our harvest of the climate finance course syllabi findable on the internet did not bring it up. Climate finance took shape recently, in thousands of expert reports, institutional decisions and scattered articles, in several languages -- a literature too vast and too fragmented for a single reader to hold entire. We build an empirical corpus that aims at exhaustiveness, in which institutional publications take an important place. This matches the nature of the object under study: climate finance is not defined by a single theory published in a small set of Anglo-American academic outputs.

![Growth of the climate finance literature, 1990--2023. The field is nearly nonexistent before 2007, structures itself after Bali and Copenhagen, then develops within the categories stabilised after Paris.](figures/fig_bars_v1.png){#fig-bars width=85%}

The historical argument covers the period 1990--2025. The systematic bibliographic corpus stops at 2024, the last complete year available at the time of the analysis; the year 2025 enters through the Türkiye case, as a contemporary test of the stabilised categories.

The computational analysis of about {{< meta corpus_total_approx >}} works is discussed in the appendix. The results provide convergent clues: the lexical breaks, the co-citation communities and the thematic recomposition of the corpus are consistent with a periodization in three moments: before climate finance (1990--2006), crystallization (2007--2014), an established and contested field (2015--2024). Thus @fig-breaks shows the speed at which the themes treated in the literature renew themselves. The index is expressed in relative units and computed with no prior knowledge of the periodization. The results support our reading in two breaks. The lexicographic shifts tabulated in the appendix show, between periods, the retreat of terms tied to the Clean Development Mechanism and the Kyoto vocabulary, and the advance of terms tied to mobilised finance, funds, flows, aid and, later, green finance.

![Speed of thematic renewal in the climate finance literature: the field went through two spells of faster change. See the qualitative recomposition of the themes treated in appendix A.5.](figures/fig_breaks.png){#fig-breaks width=85%}
